text: Two Alice forceps are applied on either side of the inferior crease of the umbilicus. A small stab incision is then made in the inferior crease of the umbilicus.

The surgeon lifts the abdominal wall and inserts a Veress needle, performing irrigation, suction, and hanging drop tests to confirm placement. The insufflator is set to pediatric mode, and the tube is attached to the Veress needle. Gas flow is adjusted to 0.1 liters per year of age, resulting in uniform abdominal distension.
The surgeon removes the varus needle after completing insufflation and enlarges the stab incision at the umbilicus to an 11 millimeter incision.

The surgeon carefully introduces the trocar by holding its tip, with the index finger guarding it, and listens for the hissing noise of air. The insufflation tube is connected to the trocar, and the first umbilical port is introduced, allowing the telescope to be inserted for laparoscopic visualization.
The surgeon performs an initial diagnostic laparoscopy, visually inspecting the abdominal cavity using a laparoscopic camera inserted through the umbilical port.

The surgeon introduces a second 7mm working port through the left iliac fossa and a third 5mm working port through the suprapubic area, with external camera visuals showing the port locations on the abdominal surface.

The laparoscopic view shows the cecum, ileum, ileocecal junction, and an inflamed appendix adherent to the lateral abdominal wall. The surgeon performs a gentle blunt dissection to separate the appendix from the abdominal wall, noting the absence of dense adhesions due to the acute nature of the case.
The surgeon performs suction irrigation to clear the surgical field, removing fluids and debris for better visibility of the anatomical structures.
The surgeon uses bipolar cautery and scissors to separate the mesoappendix from the appendix, cauterizing close to the appendix to minimize bleeding due to the small vessels in the area.
The surgeon lifts the appendix to prevent the diathermy from contacting surrounding bowel loops. In pediatric cases, the mesoappendix is minimal due to less fat.
The surgeon uses suction to remove minor oozing from the surgical field, followed by irrigation to clear the area.

The surgeon skeletonizes the appendix up to its base, using irrigation and suction to clear the field. The mesoappendix is positioned away, allowing the appendix to be twisted for better access, reaching the base near the cecum.
The tinea coli is visible, guiding the surgeon to the base of the appendix. An extracorporeal endo loop is introduced and secured at the base of the appendix using a knot pusher.

The surgeon introduces a second endo loop and secures it three millimeters distal to the first loop at the base of the appendix. A third endo loop is then introduced and secured six millimeters distal to the second loop, effectively preventing spillage of fecolith from the appendix.
The surgeon cuts the appendix between the second and third endo loops, leaving a minimum three-millimeter stem to secure the knot. The appendix is then removed through a seven-millimeter port.
The surgeon performs a thorough wash of the surgical area, including the right anterior flank (RAF), Morrison's pouch, and the pelvis, using irrigation and suction.
